title: Journal prompts as practice
subtitle: Single-question free-writes, against a timer, with no editing.
excerpt: A reference article for the journal prompt practices in the library. Covers the free-write style, why prompts are specific questions rather than blank pages, and how to work a prompt set in fifteen to thirty minutes.
sourceNotes: The single-question free-write style is the one Rebecca J Page uses across The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (2025), which holds 84 daily prompts. The wider free-write method has multiple lineages, most prominently Julia Cameron's morning pages (The Artist's Way, 1992) and Peter Elbow's freewriting (Writing Without Teachers, 1973); the version used here uses a timer and a single question rather than open pages.

practice.whenToUse: Read once before working any of the journal prompt sets in the library.

glossaryTerms.definition: Writing for a fixed period without stopping, editing, or re-reading. The pen keeps moving even when the page goes blank.

content.text: Journal prompts in the library are specific questions, not blank pages. Each prompt names one thing to write about. You write for five minutes without stopping, don't re-read or edit, and move on. A prompt set holds two to six prompts that walk a single feeling through; fifteen to thirty minutes for a full set.

Set a timer for five minutes before each prompt. Read the prompt once. Start writing. Don't stop, don't re-read, don't edit. If the page goes blank, keep the pen moving: write "I don't know what to write" until something else comes. When the timer ends, close the notebook. Move to the next prompt or stop for the day.
The timer matters. It tells the body that the writing has a stop point, and that loosens the writing. Without it, the page becomes another open-ended task and the writing stays cautious.

The no-editing rule matters too. Re-reading interrupts the work. Most of the useful realisation in a free-write lands two or three days after the writing, not at the end of the page. Close the notebook; come back to it later if at all.
Why specific questions, not blank pages

A blank-page prompt ("How do you feel about money?") gives the head too much room to perform. A specific question ("When did 'we never get ahead' first become true for my family?") points the writing at a particular memory, story, or sentence. The specificity gets past the rehearsed answer.

Most prompt sets in the library open with one or two wider questions ("What does money feel like to me right now?") before the narrower ones ("Who in my life would have called a steady bank balance a problem?"). The warm-up orients the writing. Going straight to a narrow question can be too much too fast.
What to do with what you wrote

Most of what comes up in a free-write doesn't need anything done with it. The writing is the practice. Some prompts will bring up a line that catches you: a name, a phrase, a memory that lands sharper than the rest. That line is worth coming back to. Open the notebook to that page next week and write for five more minutes on that one line.
When a journal session is paired with tapping, write first; the line that catches becomes the named feeling in the tapping set-up statement, and the rounds work it through.

A notebook and a pen. A timer (any phone). Somewhere you won't be interrupted for fifteen to thirty minutes. Most users keep one notebook for Mindset journal work; some keep separate notebooks per topic. Both work.

Contributed by Rebecca Page. The single-question, timed, no-editing free-write is the method used across The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (2025), which holds 84 daily prompts and 12 weekly reflections. The wider lineage of free-writing as a practice has multiple sources; Julia Cameron's morning pages in The Artist's Way (1992) and Peter Elbow's freewriting (Writing Without Teachers, 1973) are the most prominent in English-language writing instruction.
